metadata.title: Additional Final Exam Sample Questions — grouped by topics
metadata.description: Structured quiz dataset converted from the uploaded final exam sample questions PDF.
metadata.note: There are 174 original questions. To keep every quiz at exactly 30 questions, the final quiz includes 6 review repeats marked with isReviewRepeat=true.

topicIndex.title: k-NN and distance-based classification
topicIndex.description: Feature scaling, k value, categorical encoding, pipelines, and leakage in k-NN.

topics.title: k-NN and distance-based classification
topics.description: Feature scaling, k value, categorical encoding, pipelines, and leakage in k-NN.

questions.prompt: You apply k-NN to a dataset with features: age (years) and salary (USD). Salary ranges from 1000 to 100000, age from 18 to 60. What will most likely happen without preprocessing?

options.text: Model will give equal importance to both features
Salary will dominate distance calculations and bias predictions

questions.answer: Salary will dominate distance calculations and bias predictions
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Salary will dominate distance calculations and bias predictions
questions.correctAnswer: Salary will dominate distance calculations and bias predictions

questions.prompt: You train a k-NN classifier with k=1 and observe 100% accuracy on training data but poor test performance. What is the most likely issue?

options.text: Overfitting to noise in training data

questions.answer: Overfitting to noise in training data
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Overfitting to noise in training data
questions.correctAnswer: Overfitting to noise in training data

questions.prompt: You split your dataset incorrectly and include test data in training. What happens?

options.text: Evaluation becomes overly optimistic due to data leakage

questions.answer: Evaluation becomes overly optimistic due to data leakage
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Evaluation becomes overly optimistic due to data leakage
questions.correctAnswer: Evaluation becomes overly optimistic due to data leakage

questions.prompt: In the code below, what is missing before training k-NN? X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.2) knn.fit(X_train, y_train)
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Feature scaling such as StandardScaler

questions.prompt: What does this code create?knn = KNeighborsClassifier(n_neighbors=5)

options.text: A k-NN classifier using 5 nearest neighbors

questions.answer: A k-NN classifier using 5 nearest neighbors
questions.explanation: Correct answer: A k-NN classifier using 5 nearest neighbors
questions.correctAnswer: A k-NN classifier using 5 nearest neighbors

questions.prompt: What is the main problem in this code? scaler = StandardScaler() X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X) X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X_scaled, y)

options.text: The scaler should not be used with k-NN
The labels should be scaled too
The model should be trained before splitting
Data leakage because scaling is done before train-test split

questions.answer: Data leakage because scaling is done before train-test split
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Data leakage because scaling is done before train-test split
questions.correctAnswer: Data leakage because scaling is done before train-test split

questions.prompt: What is the likely issue with this code for k-NN? X = df[['age', 'income', 'city']] knn.fit(X_train, y_train)

options.text: Age cannot be used in ML
Target variable is missing from X
City is categorical and must be encoded before using k-NN

questions.answer: City is categorical and must be encoded before using k-NN
questions.explanation: Correct answer: City is categorical and must be encoded before using k-NN
questions.correctAnswer: City is categorical and must be encoded before using k-NN

questions.prompt: What does this pipeline help prevent? pipe = Pipeline([ ('scaler', StandardScaler()), ('knn', KNeighborsClassifier(n_neighbors=5))]),

options.text: Incorrect preprocessing and data leakage during model training

questions.answer: Incorrect preprocessing and data leakage during model training
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Incorrect preprocessing and data leakage during model training
questions.correctAnswer: Incorrect preprocessing and data leakage during model training

studyTips: Remember: k-NN depends on distance, so scaling is critical.
k=1 often memorizes noise and overfits.
Categorical features must be encoded before using distance-based models.

topics.title: Regression and cross-validation
topics.description: Regression goals, linear regression, coefficients, k-fold CV, model selection, MSE, and multicollinearity.

questions.prompt: What is the main goal of regression?
Which of the following is a regression task?
What does a linear regression model assume?
What is k-fold cross-validation?
Why is cross-validation useful?
What is a drawback of cross-validation?
You add many irrelevant features to a regression model. What is the likely effect?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Model may overfit noise

questions.prompt: You compare two models using cross-validation. Model A has stable scores, Model B has highly varying scores. What does this suggest?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Model B is unstable

questions.prompt: You use k-fold cross-validation with k=2. What is a drawback?
You use cross-validation to choose between models. What is the main goal?
You fit regression with highly correlated features and notice large coefficient values. Why?
You apply linear regression without intercept on shifted data. What happens?
What does this code measure?mean_squared_error(y_test, y_pred)
What does this pipeline help prevent?pipe = Pipeline([ ('scaler', StandardScaler()), ('model', LinearRegression())])cross_val_score(pipe, X, y, cv=5)
What does this code display?pd.DataFrame({ 'feature': X.columns, 'coef': model.coef_})

studyTips: Regression predicts numeric values.
Cross-validation gives a more stable estimate than one split.
Watch out for multicollinearity when coefficients become unstable.

topics.title: Regularization: Ridge, LASSO, Elastic Net
topics.description: L1/L2 penalties, lambda strength, high-dimensional overfitting, and coefficient shrinkage.

questions.prompt: Which method combines LASSO and Ridge?
What does this code do?Ridge(alpha=1.0)
What does this code do?Lasso(alpha=0.1)
Why is regularization useful in high-dimensional data?
What does λ (lambda) control in regularization?
What happens when λ is very large?
What happens when λ = 0?

studyTips: Ridge = L2, LASSO = L1, Elastic Net = both.
Large lambda shrinks coefficients more strongly.
Regularization mainly fights overfitting.

topics.title: Classification metrics and logistic regression
topics.description: Accuracy, precision, recall, confusion matrix, sigmoid, thresholds, AUC, scaling, convergence, and L1 solver issues.

questions.prompt: What does accuracy measure?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: All correct predictions ratio

questions.prompt: Given TP=50, TN=40, FP=10, FN=0, what is accuracy?
What is precision?

options.text: TP divided by all actual positives

questions.explanation: Correct answer: TP divided by predicted positives

questions.prompt: What function is used in logistic regression?
What is the sigmoid function output range?
What is the decision threshold in logistic regression?
What is AUC?
AUC = 0.5 means?
AUC close to 1 means?
What happens if FP increases?
What happens if FN increases?
Given confusion matrix [[50,10],[5,35]], what is accuracy?
You run logistic regression and get this warning:ConvergenceWarning: lbfgs failed to convergeWhat is the most likely fix?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Increase max_iter or scale features

questions.prompt: What is the issue in this code?model = LogisticRegression()model.fit(X_train, y_train)print(model.coef_)Features are on very different scales

options.text: No issue, coefficients are always comparable
Coefficients are not comparable without scaling

questions.answer: Coefficients are not comparable without scaling
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Coefficients are not comparable without scaling
questions.correctAnswer: Coefficients are not comparable without scaling

questions.prompt: You run logistic regression on perfectly separable data. What happens?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Coefficients may grow very large

questions.prompt: What is the issue here?model = LogisticRegression(penalty='l1')model.fit(X_train, y_train)

options.text: L1 cannot be used with classification

questions.explanation: Correct answer: Solver must support L1 penalty

questions.prompt: You run this code:model = LogisticRegression()model.fit(X_train, y_train)y_pred = (model.predict_proba(X_test)[:,1] > 0.3)What is the main effect?
What is the issue here? Dataset is highly imbalanced model.fit(X_train, y_train)y_pred = model.predict(X_test)accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred)
What does this code do? LogisticRegression(class_weight='balanced')
What happens if distance threshold is very small?
You use PCA for classification but accuracy drops. What is the likely reason?

studyTips: Precision focuses on predicted positives; recall focuses on actual positives.
AUC near 0.5 is random; near 1 is strong.
Lower threshold usually increases recall.

topics.title: Decision trees and information gain
topics.description: Random state, overfitting, validation, GridSearchCV, max_depth, entropy, and information gain.

questions.prompt: What happens in this code?tree = DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=42)tree.fit(X_train, y_train)tree2 = DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=42)tree2.fit(X_train, y_train)
Why set random_state in training and splitting?
What is the issue here?tree.fit(X_train, y_train)print(tree.score(X_train, y_train))Score is 1.00.
What is the better next check after training score 1.00?
What is the issue in this code?grid = GridSearchCV(tree, param_grid, cv=5)grid.fit(X, y)print(grid.best_score_)
questions.explanation: Correct answer: No separate holdout test

questions.prompt: What does this code most likely cause?tree = DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=None, min_samples_leaf=1)tree.fit(X_train, y_train)
What is the hidden issue in this code?scores = cross_val_score(tree, X_test, y_test, cv=5)

options.text: Validation is done on holdout data

questions.answer: Validation is done on holdout data
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Validation is done on holdout data
questions.correctAnswer: Validation is done on holdout data

questions.prompt: What split is preferred in decision trees?
A node has entropy 1. After split, weighted entropy is 0.4. What is information gain?
If a split does not change entropy, what happens?
What happens if all splits give low information gain?
What is the effect of noisy data on entropy-based splits?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Tree may overfit noise

questions.prompt: What happens if max_depth is not limited in entropy-based trees?
What happens if tree grows without limits?
Which is a hyperparameter in decision trees?
What does each tree in Random Forest use?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Random subset of data

studyTips: Unlimited trees can overfit easily.
Information gain = parent entropy - weighted child entropy.
Use validation/test scores, not only training score.

topics.title: Train/test split, validation, and preprocessing
topics.description: Train-test split, holdout sets, validation sets, hyperparameters, categorical data, one-hot encoding, polynomial features, and missing data.

questions.prompt: What is the purpose of train_test_split?
What is a good practice before splitting data?
What is the issue if data is not shuffled?
What happens if we tune hyperparameters on test data?
What is a hyperparameter?
Why do we keep a holdout test set separate from validation?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: To get unbiased evaluation

questions.prompt: What is the main role of the validation set?
Why is data preprocessing important before modeling?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: To improve data quality

questions.prompt: What is the main issue with categorical features in ML models?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: They are not numeric

questions.prompt: What does one-hot encoding do?
What is a drawback of one-hot encoding?
What happens if polynomial degree is too low?
What does this code do?PolynomialFeatures(degree=2)
What is the output of polynomial features degree=2 for one feature x?
What is the correct approach to avoid leakage with polynomial features?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Use pipeline with split

questions.prompt: What is the main problem with missing data?
Which is a simple method to handle missing values?
When is dropping rows with missing values risky?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Too much data removed

questions.prompt: What is mean imputation best for?
What is the issue in this code? X_filled = df.fillna(df.mean()) X_train, X_test = train_test_split(X_filled)
What does this code return?Z.shape
You set n_components=1 but model performance drops. Why?
What happens if you increase n_components?
What is the correct reconstruction?
What happens if data is very sparse?

studyTips: Fit preprocessing only on training data to avoid leakage.
Validation selects hyperparameters; test set gives final unbiased estimate.
One-hot encoding turns categories into binary columns.

topics.title: Imbalanced data handling
topics.description: Undersampling, oversampling, SMOTE, class weights, misleading accuracy, F1 and recall.

questions.prompt: What is a drawback of undersampling?
What is a drawback of oversampling?
What does SMOTE do?
What is the correct fix for imbalanced evaluation?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Use F1 or recall

studyTips: Accuracy can lie when classes are imbalanced.
Use recall/F1 when minority class matters.
Oversampling can overfit; undersampling can lose information.

questions.prompt: What is a key difference between bagging and boosting?

options.text: Bagging reduces bias, boosting reduces variance
Bagging uses weights, boosting does not
Bagging trains models independently, boosting sequentially

questions.answer: Bagging trains models independently, boosting sequentially
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Bagging trains models independently, boosting sequentially
questions.correctAnswer: Bagging trains models independently, boosting sequentially

studyTips: Bagging trains models independently; boosting trains sequentially.
Random Forest is the classic bagging example.
Boosting can overfit with too many iterations or high learning rate.

topics.title: Unsupervised learning and KMeans
topics.description: Clustering goals, KMeans, scaling, centroids, distribution shift, silhouette score, outliers, and anomaly distances.

questions.prompt: What is the goal of clustering?
Which algorithm is used for clustering?
Why is scaling important in KMeans?
What is the issue in this code?kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=3)kmeans.fit(X_scaled)kmeans.fit(X_test)
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Test data used again

questions.prompt: You run KMeans with very large K (close to number of samples). What happens?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Each point becomes cluster

questions.prompt: What is the issue in this code?kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=3)kmeans.fit(X)centers = kmeans.cluster_centers_kmeans.fit(X_new)
You apply KMeans on dataset with many irrelevant features. What happens?
You use KMeans for customer segmentation, but clusters change significantly after adding new data. Why?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Centroids depend on data distribution

questions.prompt: You run this pipeline on customer data:pipe = Pipeline([('scaler', StandardScaler()),('kmeans', KMeans(n_clusters=4))])pipe.fit(X_train)labels = pipe.predict(X_test)Clusters on test data look completely different from training. What is the most likely cause?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Train and test distributions differ

questions.prompt: You run KMeans and then evaluate clusters like this:labels = kmeans.fit_predict(X_scaled)score = silhouette_score(X_scaled, labels)You get a high score, but clusters are useless in business. Why?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Metric ignores business meaning

questions.prompt: You cluster data and then add a new feature:kmeans.fit(X_old)labels_old = kmeans.labels_kmeans.fit(X_new)labels_new = kmeans.labels_Labels change drastically. Why?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Feature space changed distances

questions.prompt: You run KMeans with this code:kmeans = KMeans(n_clusters=3, random_state=42)labels = kmeans.fit_predict(X_scaled)You notice one cluster has only 2 points while others have thousands. What does this suggest?
You want to use KMeans for anomaly detection:kmeans.fit(X_scaled)distances = kmeans.transform(X_scaled)Which idea is correct?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Large distances indicate anomalies

questions.prompt: Optimal number of clusters in silhouette method is:
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Maximizing average silhouette score

studyTips: KMeans needs scaling because it uses distances.
Very large K can make each point its own cluster.
Silhouette can be mathematically good but business-useless.

topics.title: DBSCAN density-based clustering
topics.description: eps, min_samples, noise label -1, no predict method, silhouette issues, and cluster counting.

questions.prompt: You run DBSCAN and all points are labeled as noise (-1). What is the most likely issue?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: eps is too small

questions.prompt: You run DBSCAN and get only one large cluster. What is the most likely cause?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: eps is too large

questions.prompt: You increase min_samples significantly. What happens?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: More points become noise

questions.prompt: What is the issue in this code? db = DBSCAN(eps=0.5, min_samples=5); db.fit(X_train); labels = db.fit_predict(X_test)
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Second fit overwrites model

questions.prompt: You try to use DBSCAN like this:db.fit(X_train)labels = db.predict(X_test)What is the issue?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: DBSCAN has no predict

questions.prompt: You run DBSCAN and then compute silhouette score:labels = db.fit_predict(X_scaled)silhouette_score(X_scaled, labels)You get an error. Why?
You try to tune DBSCAN like this:for eps in [0.1, 0.5, 1.0]: db = DBSCAN(eps=eps) labels = db.fit_predict(X_scaled) print(len(set(labels)))What is the flaw?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Noise counted as cluster

questions.prompt: How to fix cluster counting in DBSCAN?
You run DBSCAN and get only noise. Then you scale data differently and clusters appear. Why?
What is DBSCAN used for?
What is a core point in DBSCAN?

options.text: Point with at least MinPts neighbors

questions.answer: Point with at least MinPts neighbors
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Point with at least MinPts neighbors
questions.correctAnswer: Point with at least MinPts neighbors

studyTips: Small eps: many/noise points; large eps: one big cluster.
DBSCAN labels noise as -1.
Standard DBSCAN has no predict method for new points.

topics.title: Hierarchical clustering and silhouette basics
topics.description: Linkage, dendrograms, fcluster, distance thresholds, single linkage, silhouette method, agglomerative complexity.

questions.prompt: What does this code produce?Z = linkage(X, method='ward')
What does this code visualize?dendrogram(Z)
What does this code do?fcluster(Z, t=5, criterion='distance')
You use method='single' in linkage. What happens?
Time complexity of naive agglomerative clustering is:

studyTips: Linkage builds hierarchy; dendrogram visualizes it.
Small distance threshold creates many clusters.
Single linkage can produce chain-like clusters.

topics.title: PCA and dimensionality reduction
topics.description: PCA leakage, train-only fitting, components, explained variance, n_components, and information loss.

questions.prompt: What is the issue in this code?pca = PCA(n_components=2)X_pca = pca.fit_transform(X)X_train, X_test = train_test_split(X_pca)

options.text: X must be scaled after PCA

questions.explanation: Correct answer: PCA applied before split

questions.prompt: What is the correct workflow to avoid leakage with PCA?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Fit PCA on train only

questions.prompt: What is the issue here?scaler = StandardScaler()X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)pca = PCA(n_components=2)pca.fit(X_scaled)X_test_pca = pca.transform(X_test)
What does this code return?pca.components_
What does this code return?pca.explained_variance_ratio_
You run PCA and get:explained_variance_ratio_ = [0.9, 0.05, 0.05]What does this mean?
What is the issue here?pca = PCA(n_components=0.95)X_pca = pca.fit_transform(X_scaled)
questions.explanation: Correct answer: No issue, valid usage

questions.prompt: What does n_components=0.95 mean?
What is the key difference between LDA and PCA?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: LDA uses class labels

questions.prompt: What is PCA mainly used for?
What is dimensionality reduction?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Reducing number of features

studyTips: Fit PCA on train only to avoid leakage.
More components preserve more variance.
Too few components can lose important information.

topics.title: LDA and SVD
topics.description: LDA vs PCA, class separation, covariance, singular values, TruncatedSVD, and pipeline order.

questions.prompt: What is the main goal of LDA?
What is a key difference between PCA and SVD?
What happens if you keep only top singular values?
What is the issue in this pipeline?pipe = Pipeline([('svd', TruncatedSVD(n_components=2)),('scaler', StandardScaler())])
What does positive covariance indicate?
What is truncated SVD?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Reduced feature matrix factorization

studyTips: LDA uses labels and tries to separate classes.
PCA is variance-based; SVD is matrix factorization.
TruncatedSVD is useful for dimensionality reduction.

topics.title: NMF: Nonnegative Matrix Factorization
topics.description: Non-negative constraint, factorization, reconstruction, sparse data, MinMaxScaler, and information loss.

questions.prompt: What is the main constraint in NMF?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: All values must be non-negative

questions.prompt: What is the goal of NMF?
What is the issue in this code?model = NMF(n_components=2)X_new = model.fit_transform(X)X contains negative values
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Negative values not allowed

questions.prompt: What is the issue in this pipeline?pipe = Pipeline([('scaler', StandardScaler()),('nmf', NMF(n_components=2))])
What is the correct approach for pipeline with NMF?
What does NMF stand for?
What type of technique is NMF?
Why may NMF lose more information than PCA?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Due to non-negativity constraint

questions.prompt: What is the optimization method used in NMF?

studyTips: NMF requires non-negative values.
Use MinMaxScaler rather than StandardScaler before NMF.
NMF factorizes data into parts-based components.
